nativeRange.habitat: Lower Rio Tapajós and Rio Arapiuns tributaries; clear-water, sandy-bottomed reaches of these eastern Amazon affluents, often near flooded forest margins and submerged wood

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (mandatory — minimum 5 cm depth for sifting behaviour)
smooth river stones and driftwood as territory markers
submerged roots and branches mimicking flooded-forest margins
robust or potted background plants (Amazon swords, Vallisneria); avoid delicate carpeting plants likely to be uprooted by sifting
open sand foraging areas in front, sheltered refuge zones behind decor
dim to moderate lighting; floating plants can be used to diffuse light

diet.notes: In the wild, the Red Head Tapajos Eartheater feeds by taking mouthfuls of fine sand and sieving invertebrates, insect larvae, small crustaceans, and organic detritus through specialised gill rakers, expelling the sand from the operculum — a feeding style known as benthophagous sand-sifting. In an aquarium it readily accepts high-quality sinking pellets or granules, frozen and live bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, and daphnia; some vegetable matter such as spirulina or blanched greens is beneficial. Feeding three to four smaller portions per day rather than a single large meal is generally advised. Gravel substrate must be avoided — coarse particles damage the fine gill rakers used in sifting.

goodWith: other peaceful South American cichlids of similar size (Satanoperca, other Geophagus, Biotodoma)
large peaceful tetras (Bleeding Heart, Congo, Silver Dollars)
robust open-water characins (Hemiodus, Anostomus where space allows)
peaceful loricariid catfish (Ancistrus, Hypancistrus) and large Corydoras
Discus and angelfish in suitably large, warm soft-water tanks
avoidWith: very small fish that could be predated on during sand-sifting (shrimp, fry)
aggressive or large territorial cichlids (Oscar, Midas, Red Devil) — will be bullied
fast, voracious feeders that outcompete eartheaters at the substrate
delicate substrate-rooting plants likely to be uprooted
small shrimp and snails (taken during sifting)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Mattia Nocciola; 6000x4000. Sharp, whole-fish profile of a live adult in a display aquarium showing the diagnostic vivid red-orange head, iridescent orange-silver body with blue spangling and dark lateral blotch. Best colour/ID match.

Photographer Ong Jyh Seng; ~1024px. Sharp full-body live individual, correct ID with vertical bars and lateral spot, but head colour is less-developed/washed under blue tank lighting so not the lead pick.

summary: The Red Head Tapajos Eartheater is a striking South American cichlid from the lower Rio Tapajós in Brazil, formally described as Geophagus pyrocephalus in 2021. Adults reach 15–22 cm and are widely kept in the hobby for their vivid red-orange head colouration. A group of five or more and a fine sand substrate are essential.
careNotes:
Geophagus pyrocephalus was formally described in 2021 and is the species long sold in the hobby as 'Geophagus sp. Red Head Tapajós' or 'Geophagus sp. Orange Head'. Adults develop a vivid red-orange flush across the front of the head, contrasting with an iridescent silvery body marked with vertical bars, and the species is widely kept and sought after in the aquarium trade. It is closely related to, but distinct from, the larger Geophagus altifrons; most modern hobby stock is aquacultured in Asia.

Fine sand substrate is non-negotiable. Like other Geophagus, this species feeds by taking mouthfuls of substrate, filtering invertebrates and organic matter through specialised gill rakers, and expelling the sand from the operculum. Sharp gravel particles physically damage these rakers, leading to chronic injury and secondary infection. A minimum sand bed of about 5 cm is recommended; a deeper bed (8 cm+) is preferred for sustained sifting behaviour.

A tank of at least roughly 400 L with a 150 cm or longer footprint is generally advised for a group of 5–8 adults. Smaller tanks (around 190 L / 50 US gallons) are sometimes recommended for young fish in trade listings, but the adult footprint and grouping requirements push the long-term home toward the larger figure. Keep in soft, warm, slightly acidic to neutral water (around 25–29°C, pH ~5.5–7.5, gH 1–12) and maintain low nitrate via regular large water changes — this species is reported to be sensitive to deteriorating water conditions.

Generally considered a peaceful cichlid that will not predate on even quite small tankmates outside of spawning periods. It is gregarious and should be kept in groups of at least 5 (ideally 6–8 or more) to spread out minor intraspecific aggression and bring out natural social behaviour. Avoid keeping single specimens. Pairs that form within a group can become temporarily territorial during spawning.
breedingNotes:
Geophagus pyrocephalus is a biparental, larvophilous (delayed) mouthbrooder: eggs are laid on a cleaned flat surface (rock or firm substrate) and fertilised externally; both parents guard the clutch on the substrate, then take the wriggling larvae into the buccal cavity after hatching (reported at roughly 48–72 hours). Both male and female take turns mouthbrooding the larvae for a further period until they are free-swimming.

Conditioning a group with varied live and frozen foods, soft slightly acidic water and stable warm temperatures is generally advised before breeding. Pairs typically form spontaneously within a larger group. Fry can be raised in the main tank if sufficient cover exists, or transferred to a rearing tank after free-swimming; suitable first foods include baby brine shrimp and finely crushed dry foods.

primary.name: Aqua Imports — Red Head Tapajos Eartheater
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Provided scientific name (Geophagus pyrocephalus), adult size (~6 in / ~15 cm trade size), temperature 76–82°F (24.4–27.8°C), pH 6.5–7.5, tank size 50 gal+, omnivorous diet, peaceful temperament, group size 5+, native range (lower Rio Tapajós, Brazil), aquacultured Asian origin, sand-sifting requirement, and characteristic red-orange head colouration.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Geophagus sp. 'orange head'
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes/Cichliformes), adult size 200–250 mm SL, temperature 26–30°C, pH 4.5–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dH), recommended footprint 180×60 cm (corresponds to ~600 L+), benthophagous diet with 3–4 small feedings per day, peaceful temperament outside breeding, group size 5–8 minimum, native range (lower Tapajós + Rio Arapiuns), and biparental delayed-mouthbrooding mode with ~72 h hatch. Size source disagreement: SF gives up to 25 cm SL while Aqua Imports lists ~15 cm trade size and Fishkeeper 18 cm — averaged to 15–22 cm range.

additional.name: Fishkeeper (UK) — Redhead Tapajos Eartheater
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~18 cm / 7.1 in), temperature 25–29°C, pH 5.0–7.0, hardness up to 12 dH, omnivorous diet (flake/granules/cichlid pellets, frozen mosquito larvae and brine shrimp), peaceful temperament in groups, group size 6+, native range (Rio Tapajós, Brazil), biparental mouthbrooding with eggs hatching in 48–72 h and fry free-swimming after 8–10 days. Tank dimensions not stated; size and water-parameter values consistent with the averaged figures used here.

fish.breedingNotes: The Red Head Tapajos Eartheater is a biparental, larvophilous mouthbrooder, meaning the eggs are laid and fertilised on a flat surface before the hatched larvae — rather than the eggs themselves — are taken into the parents' mouths for protection. Eggs typically hatch in around 48 to 72 hours, after which both parents take turns holding the larvae; fry become free-swimming after approximately 8 to 10 days. Pairs form spontaneously within a group of five or more. Soft, warm, slightly acidic water and a varied conditioning diet of live and frozen foods are generally advised before breeding.
